message.content: check the logs on my computer to see why my computer went to sleep sometime in the last 2 days if it is set to never sleep.
check the logs on my computer to see why my computer went to sleep sometime in the last 2 days if it is set to never sleep.

content.text: I'll check the Windows power/sleep event logs on your machine. Let me load the tools I need.

content.content:
{"result":"Response: Power Scheme GUID: 448909a7-70e1-4560-a8cd-11c459a98a3a  (albert)
  Subgroup GUID: 238c9fa8-0aad-41ed-83f4-97be242c8f20  (Sleep)
    GUID Alias: SUB_SLEEP
    Power Setting GUID: 29f6c1db-86da-48c5-9fdb-f2b67b1f44da  (Sleep after)
      GUID Alias: STANDBYIDLE
      Minimum Possible Setting: 0x00000000
      Maximum Possible Setting: 0xffffffff
      Possible Settings increment: 0x00000001
      Possible Settings units: Seconds
    Current AC Power Setting Index: 0x00000000
    Current DC Power Setting Index: 0x000000b4


Status Code: 0"}

structuredContent.result:
Response: Power Scheme GUID: 448909a7-70e1-4560-a8cd-11c459a98a3a  (albert)
  Subgroup GUID: 238c9fa8-0aad-41ed-83f4-97be242c8f20  (Sleep)
    GUID Alias: SUB_SLEEP
    Power Setting GUID: 29f6c1db-86da-48c5-9fdb-f2b67b1f44da  (Sleep after)
      GUID Alias: STANDBYIDLE
      Minimum Possible Setting: 0x00000000
      Maximum Possible Setting: 0xffffffff
      Possible Settings increment: 0x00000001
      Possible Settings units: Seconds
    Current AC Power Setting Index: 0x00000000
    Current DC Power Setting Index: 0x000000b4


Status Code: 0

content.text: Never-sleep is confirmed on AC power (Sleep after = 0). On battery it's 3 min. Now the sleep/wake events from the last 2 days.

input.command: $start=(Get-Date).AddDays-2 2>$null; $ev=Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System'; Id=42,1,107,506,507,566; StartTime=(Get-Date).AddDays(-2)} -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Sort-Object TimeCreated; foreach($e in $ev){ "{0} | Id={1} | {2} | {3}" -f $e.TimeCreated, $e.Id, $e.ProviderName, ($e.Message -replace "`r`n"," " ).Substring(0,[Math]::Min(180,($e.Message).Length)) }

content.text: The log has the answer but I need the sleep-entry details. Let me pull the Kernel-Power (ID 42, sleep entered) and Power-Troubleshooter (wake source / sleep reason) events with full text.

input.command: $ev=Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System'; ProviderName='Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Power','Microsoft-Windows-Power-Troubleshooter'; StartTime=(Get-Date).AddDays(-3)} -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Sort-Object TimeCreated; foreach($e in $ev){ "==== {0} | Id={1} | {2} ====`n{3}`n" -f $e.TimeCreated,$e.Id,$e.ProviderName,($e.Message) }

content.text:
Found it. Your computer didn't sleep because of an idle timer — it was forced to sleep by a **critically low battery** while unplugged.

Here's the exact sequence from the event log, on **July 5 at 4:32:50 AM**:

- `Kernel-Power ID 524` — "Critical Battery Trigger Met"
- `Kernel-Power ID 566` — "Austerity... Battery Drain Budget Exceeded"
- `Kernel-Power ID 42` — "The system is entering sleep. **Sleep Reason: Battery**"

It then stayed in low-power state until you woke it with the **power button on July 6 at 10:45 AM** (`Power-Troubleshooter ID 1`: Sleep 7/5 → Wake 7/6, Wake Source: Power Button). That ~30-hour gap also showed up as a big clock-resync event when it woke.

Why "never sleep" didn't prevent it: that setting only governs the **idle sleep timer**, and on your machine it's only set to Never on **AC power (plugged in)**. On battery, your sleep-after is 3 minutes — but more importantly, the **critical battery action** overrides everything. When the battery drops to the critical threshold, Windows forces sleep/hibernate no matter what, to avoid data loss. So the machine was unplugged, drained to critical, and put itself to sleep.

In short: it wasn't a bug or a misconfigured timer — the laptop was running on battery and hit critical charge.

Want me to change the **critical battery action** or **low-battery thresholds** (e.g., hibernate earlier, or notify sooner), or check what was draining it overnight?

result:
Found it. Your computer didn't sleep because of an idle timer — it was forced to sleep by a **critically low battery** while unplugged.

Here's the exact sequence from the event log, on **July 5 at 4:32:50 AM**:

- `Kernel-Power ID 524` — "Critical Battery Trigger Met"
- `Kernel-Power ID 566` — "Austerity... Battery Drain Budget Exceeded"
- `Kernel-Power ID 42` — "The system is entering sleep. **Sleep Reason: Battery**"

It then stayed in low-power state until you woke it with the **power button on July 6 at 10:45 AM** (`Power-Troubleshooter ID 1`: Sleep 7/5 → Wake 7/6, Wake Source: Power Button). That ~30-hour gap also showed up as a big clock-resync event when it woke.

Why "never sleep" didn't prevent it: that setting only governs the **idle sleep timer**, and on your machine it's only set to Never on **AC power (plugged in)**. On battery, your sleep-after is 3 minutes — but more importantly, the **critical battery action** overrides everything. When the battery drops to the critical threshold, Windows forces sleep/hibernate no matter what, to avoid data loss. So the machine was unplugged, drained to critical, and put itself to sleep.

In short: it wasn't a bug or a misconfigured timer — the laptop was running on battery and hit critical charge.

Want me to change the **critical battery action** or **low-battery thresholds** (e.g., hibernate earlier, or notify sooner), or check what was draining it overnight?
